input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a building (in the architecture, engineering, construction and operations domain) equipped with sensors, actuators and controllers that are modeled within a BIM environment. The building’s infrastructure components—HVAC, lighting, security, etc.—are represented as semantic BIM objects to enable a complete life‑cycle digital twin ranging from design through operation and refurbishment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin delivers real‑time data exchange for continuous synchronization, monitoring of system states for facility managers, control of physical devices via implemented control logic, interactive 3‑D visualization of building performance, and a modular extension point for custom services and analytics developed by DT engineers (e.g., predictive analytics, optimization plug‑ins).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on BIM models enriched with sensor/actuator definitions, a model‑driven engineering (MDE) based deployment framework, a modular architecture with standardized extension points, and runtime services for real‑time data exchange and visualization. The framework is demonstrated within the TwinLight and BOREALIS research projects.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The framework provides real‑time data exchange capabilities that keep the physical building and its digital counterpart continuously synchronized; however, specific update frequencies, latency limits, or temporal granularity are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: A single, centralized Digital Twin instance is managed by the DT Framework acting as a hub; building control engineers, facility managers, and DT engineers interact with this central hub, indicating a centralized architecture without explicit mention of multiple coordinated twins or hierarchical instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a modular, service‑oriented architecture with standardized extension points and runtime configuration management; the DT Framework serves as the central orchestration component, mediating bidirectional data exchange between BIM models, sensors, and custom analytics services.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external and heterogeneous systems is achieved via interoperable interfaces and standardized extension points, supporting custom service integration and analytics; the documents do not specify concrete data exchange protocols or standards such as OPC UA or MQTT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework uses enriched BIM models as the source of geometric models (3‑D representations rendered in Unreal Engine 5) and defines behavioral models through TwinComponents and FeatureParsers (e.g., LightTwinComponent and LightFeatureParser that react to on/off, brightness, and color‑temperature values). Real‑time data streams originate from sensors embedded in the BIM model and are transported via Eclipse Hono to Eclipse Ditto, which stores events persistently. Model relationships are expressed by linking each TwinComponent to its corresponding DittoDevice/DittoConnection, allowing automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts. Data management relies on Ditto’s policy‑based validation (enforcing value ranges and units) and on middleware that performs validation, conversion, and configuration before feeding data to the UE visualization services.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are modeled directly in the BIM environment and represented in the twin by LightTwinComponent instances that control lighting devices. Control commands (e.g., turn on/off, adjust brightness, set color temperature) are issued through the UnrealTwinController, which forwards messages via a DittoController to the underlying DittoDevice and DittoConnection. Remote actuation uses the Ditto API over MQTT/HTTP, with actuation ranges limited by policy‑defined value intervals and safety constraints enforced by pre‑execution checks in Ditto to prevent unsafe operations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensor components are defined in the BIM model and linked to digital counterparts that publish measurements through Eclipse Hono to Eclipse Ditto. The twin receives these updates via the DittoController, making them available to the UE5 visualization layer. While the documents do not specify sensor types, sampling frequencies, or transmission rates, the architecture supports real‑time data exchange and ensures that sensor metadata (units, ranges) is validated by Ditto policies.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is maintained by enriching BIM models with device metadata and by using validation, conversion, and configuration components before deployment. Quality assurance includes Eclipse Ditto’s policy‑based validation (checking sensor value ranges, units, and actuator command safety), pre‑execution checks for commands, and fault tolerance via Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery combined with Ditto’s persistent event storage. These mechanisms provide error detection, correction, and uncertainty mitigation throughout the twin’s lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The proposed model‑driven DT deployment framework supports a full DT lifecycle composed of (1) device modeling where Building Control Engineers enrich BIM models with explicit sensor, actuator, and controller definitions; (2) model transformation using Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) to extract a structured representation from the enriched BIM; (3) automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts (code, configuration, and service descriptors); (4) deployment and runtime operation during which real‑time data exchange synchronises the physical building and its digital counterpart; (5) interactive, immersive visualization for monitoring and control; and (6) post‑deployment evolution through the integration of custom services and analytics via standardized extension points. Governance is enforced by the modular architecture and its extension‑point contracts, which define how artifacts are versioned, validated and evolved across stages.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Twinning follows a model‑driven engineering process: Building Control Engineers model devices inside BIM, DT Engineers develop custom plugins and services, and an automated MDE pipeline extracts the enriched model to generate DT artifacts with minimal manual coding. The pipeline incorporates quality checks through real‑time data validation and supports continuous integration of new services via the framework's standardized extension points. Version control of generated artifacts and plug‑ins is implied by the modular architecture, enabling systematic evolution of the DT as operational requirements change.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The framework provides decision‑support through continuous real‑time data exchange that keeps the twin synchronized with the physical asset, and through interactive visualization interfaces that allow Facility Managers to monitor states and issue control actions. Analytics and advanced processing can be introduced by attaching custom services to the standardized extension points, thereby delivering tailored insights for operational optimisation; however, specific analytics types or confidence metrics are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Interoperability is achieved via a modular architecture with standardized extension points for plug‑in integration. The approach aligns with the connectivity and standard‑compliance strategies of open‑source DT frameworks such as Eclipse Ditto and BaSyx, which implement common IoT standards (e.g., NGSI‑LD, MQTT, OPC‑UA), although the documents do not list explicit industry standards (such as IFC, ISO 19650) or certification requirements.

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

in the IFC model. Specifically, the LightTwinComponent
component adds a UE5 light to the associated 3D representation and interprets Revit-specific photometric values (provided via device metadata in the BIM model) for accurate
light representation in the scene rendering. In addition to
managing any TwinComponent, the UnrealTwinController
brokers messages (e.g., device value updates or commands) between a component and the the corresponding ”twinned” device via the DittoController which in
turn manages the devices (cf. DittoDevice) and associated connections (cf. DittoConnection). Technically, the
UnrealTwinController relies on UE5 _Delegates_ (cf. function pointers) and implements the _Observable_ pattern [74].
Via the FeatureParserController additional behavior for

a component can be implemented. For the case of the
LightTwinComponent, we have implemented an additional
feature parser (cf. LightFeatureParser) which ”reacts” to
values such as on, brightness, and color temp kelvin

Fig. 2: Component and connector view of our model-driven DT deployment framework. The DT Framework processes
enriched BIM models and device configurations through validation, conversion, and configuration components. These feed

and updates the UE5 scene rendering accordingly. These

FeatureParser) allow for extending our proposal with additional, custom DT services inside UE5. Observe that
HVACFeatureParser and HVACTwinComponent in Figure 3 are
only for illustrative purposes and currently not implemented in
our proposal. Table IV provides a concise overview on these

Our framework ensures data integrity through Eclipse
Ditto’s policy-based validation, which enforces value ranges
and units for sensor data, and pre-execution checks for actuator commands to prevent conflicts or unsafe operations.
Fault tolerance is provided by Eclipse Hono’s at-least-once
message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, ensuring resilience against network failures or service disruptions.
These mechanisms collectively maintain synchronization and
operational safety between physical assets and their DTs.

architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform

_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time

and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
